sentences.text: But first of all, I wanted to see who was coming back.
He was hospitalized because he was sick, whatever.
My sister didn't live that time, with the baby.
And I said to myself, it's not a place for me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went-- at that time, I went to Hungary.
And in Hungary, I met a sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from my brother-in-law's sister.
One sister is on the-- on the-- on the [POLISH].
And the mother and my sister and a baby.
She went with-- with him to-- to-- to Hungary.
So I was in Romania with them for-- for a time in Bucharest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then it was a-- it was a talk Bricha going to-- to Israel.
So I said to myself, I'm going to go too.
So I went through Budapest, [BACKROUND NOISE] and I went in Budapest, Timisoara.
There it was a big town-- a nice town in-- in Hungary.
By the border to Yugoslavia was Timisoara.
Do you hear about this town?
Timisoara was a big town with-- with a-- with a-- a-- tramway, you know?
And then I joined like a group, you know, to go to Israel.
From there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed there for days.
And from there, they took us to Belgrade.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In Belgrade, I stood also.
They-- they-- they host us, in the-- the Judenrat, whatever it was.
The-- the-- the-- what do you call it, the Jewish-- what do you call it.
They-- they-- they worried about us.
And they sent us from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Yugoslavia, what is the name what I said?
From Belgrade, we went to Italy.
Belgrade, we went-- we went to a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it.
On the way-- on the way, when we came there, the English came up.
I had this letter, this Russian letter.
And I tore it away and-- and I threw it out.
This letter-- I can't-- I can't forgive myself.
I shouldn't have threw it away.
But at that time, I was afraid that, well, the English and they're watching.
They were-- if I come from Russia today, for questions.
And so they took-- I-- we went there to-- to Mestre.
From Trieste, I went to Venezia.
And I stayed in the camp in Padova.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was in-- in-- and there, I had also a-- a guy, a Jewish guy from-- from Chrzanow.
And he had a brother in Trieste, was also name of Koshitsky was [INAUDIBLE] too.
So I knew I gots a Koshitsky [LAUGHS] to rely on.
And I went to look for him.
And so I-- I-- I was in Trieste.
And I was in-- in-- in Venice.
And from Padova-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from Padova, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Milan.
At that time, there was no opening in Israel.
It was not-- it was in '45.
I just-- you wandered around, you know?
Where I sleep one night, I didn't sleep the other night.
We were always on the go.
I slept on-- on-- on-- on-- on [GERMAN], you know?
On-- on stations and the rail stations plenty times.
I washed myself in-- in-- in-- in-- in the toilets.
I didn't have no-- no place where-- where to be, with whom to be.
From my brother, I didn't hear, because he was still there.
And so I went to Milan.
From Milano, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went up to-- to Rome.
I-- I-- I did something, you know?
And I had-- I bought some-- some British, the fund, you know?
And I took them to Poland.
[LAUGHS] I went to Poland, but there was no connection-- there was no-- there was no way [LAUGHS] how to go to Poland.
And I-- and-- and-- and I didn't sleep one night without-- before-- where I slept other night.
So from there, Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sold those-- [LAUGHS] those-- those pounds, English pounds for a lot of money.
They-- I got a valise, a full valise, of the-- [LAUGHS] of the German marks.
Weiden, ?] in Germany, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a guy.
His name is the same thing as the [POLISH] Rabinowitsch.
One is in Australia and one is in Belgium.
And they were dealing and wheeling.
So I gave them the marks, and he gave me the money back in-- in Germany, and-- and-- and I went to Munich.
Munich, I found [SIGHS] relatives in Feldafing, next to the Feldafing.
Then I went back to Munich.
And I found out-- found an apartment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I live in the apartment with a friend.
This was an apartment which was requisite because the German who-- who I had an apartment, he was giving out-- he-- he was a squeak by the Germans of other Germans.
So by the end of the war, they [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] this German.
So they gave me the apartment.
When I-- then-- then I heard my brother come from-- from Poland.
So I-- I-- I-- I looked for a way to come to us, to me.
And-- and she was at that time pregnant.
And they lived in a room.
The Germans lived in one room by the beginning of the door, by the entrance.
We had the rest of the apartment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One good day, we didn't have with them peace.
They-- they-- they-- they-- they-- for-- for-- for-- for-- for-- with lies that we smuggled.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [SIGHS] by the end of it, they couldn't-- they couldn't do anything.
So one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at night [SIGHS] [BACKROUND NOISE] my sister-in-law went down.
She goes into the kitchen, and she sees three people laying on the gas, and the gas was running.
Mother, father, the one without the hand, and the daughter.
Well, it was-- was-- [SIGHS] was very dangerous.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by searching the apartment, we found a bomb in-- in-- in the pantry.
It was in the German paper.
It was full of-- full of the German paper at that time.
Me, my brother, and-- and my--
This family placed the bomb in there that would have exploded--
They placed the bomb, and they gassed themselves.
With-- with other words, they-- they wanted to see us dead.
I-- I-- I-- well, it was on [GERMAN].
I would say '46. '46, because but-- my brother was there.
There was no place where to go.
And then we opened up businesses in Munich.
We had a grocery on [GERMAN] in Munich.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also we imported textiles from Italy.
I was before in-- in Munich-- in-- in Milano, [ITALIAN].
And you see, I had connections.
[ITALIAN] and [ITALIAN] and-- and Trento, in Milano.
I was all-- all Munich, I was there.
I was-- I was traveling there.
So I had connections, and I brought in goods to-- to Germany, and we sold it there.
So it was-- it was business.
It came back, I still-- later.
Well, actually, till-- I guess-- I married here.
And when I married, I was-- I was wearing the yarmulke.
But I wasn't-- I was till-- till '52, I was in Germany.
[SIGHS] In Munich, on [GERMAN], the main synagogue.
What was his name, the rabbi?
No matter how long I lived there, but it was-- it-- the mind was temporary.
So let's put it this way.
When I had a chance to get a visa, so from time to time I got a call.
So I was-- I-- one step nearer.
Until the day came that they gave me-- they gave me the passports.
So I-- but I didn't go with-- with-- with-- what do you call it?
But I went by my own.
And on the way there, at the time from-- from Germany, I-- I traveled to Paris and I traveled to-- to-- to-- to-- to Italy, and I traveled to Holland.
My brother tended the leather business.
When I came here, we had a factory there, yeah.
You know where Jacob Street is?
Now it's the-- now the-- the-- the Brooklyn Bridge is on top of it.
Underneath was Cliff Street, Jacob Street, and William Street.
And we had-- we had a tannery.
My brother came after me right away.
Actually, my-- my-- my affidavit was to Seattle, Washington.
[LAUGHS] I didn't know where to go.
I got married and I-- I got a family, and I-- I got business [LAUGHS] and I got to tan something, you know.
Believe me, I was very busy.
Leibish-- ?] and-- and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Reuven Shmuel is Richard.
Four grandchildren, and-- and I hope more.
The other one is after their family.
I don't even remember what I told you.
The other children is Carly and Adam-- Adam-- Adam Jake.
In Jewish, it's Hannah Pesha, and he is Avraham Yisrael.
You cannot-- you cannot take it out.
I'll tell you the truth now, something else.
Today, this morning, I had a [YIDDISH].
So-- but I don't have a rabbi.
We two, another companion of mine from Krakow.
But I know-- I knew him from here.
I have a society, the Chrzanow Society--
And we have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every-- once a year, a party.
If you want to-- if you want to-- if you want to come there sometimes, you're welcome.
At that time, I didn't go just to Poland.
But I-- I had a trip, a big trip.
On the way back, my son [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has the young son, has a publishing-- had a publishing.
Now he has also a partner in something else, also Polish.
And he accumulated a travel mileage.
He gave me mileage, a lot of mileage.
I was-- I was in a lot of countries.
So I went to-- to Spain.
I went to-- I went to France.
Poland, I want-- I want to see [POLISH].
I want to see how Poland is.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What shall I say about-- what-- what shall-- what word should I say?
It's just coincidence that I-- that I got out, and that's all it is.
Because if somebody asked me, how did you come out?
I ask--I tell them, if I wouldn't be here, you-- you wouldn't got-- have nobody to ask for.
I had-- I had a lot of times [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I had depression.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to go down middle of the night and walk around here and-- and-- and couldn't get dressed.
And Prozac gives me-- I-- somehow gives me dreams which I'm very happy with it.
But I find it that to me it's-- it's-- it's relaxing.
About the stories which I told you?
But not-- not everything at once.
Well, I tell stories of how it was.
Like-- like for example, they brought people to be gassed.
They-- they knew the moment what's going to happen.
There-- there's-- there's no way of-- of not-- not realizing.
So they-- they-- they scream Shema Yisrael.
On the other side was this SS man.
Yitzraim!' ?] You know what I mean?
Yitzraim. ?] Those-- those kind of-- of-- of-- of words, there was-- there was no resent-- resentment.
At least not when-- when I-- among those-- those which I was there.
I saw them taking off the clothes.
I wasn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on top of them.
But I-- I-- I heard the-- the-- the-- the dogs barking, and they chased them.
First of all, they went there.
And then it was full, and they could-- with no place to go.
Then start the-- the-- the-- the dogs, the-- the rest of it, you know?
After-- after the gassing, there was a commander which was the [? schleppers. ?] You know?
They pulled by the hand or by the legs.
And they put on masks, and they ventilated.
Because it was impossible to be there.
And they cleaned it, because everybody-- everybody was soiled.
This is-- this is-- this is a terrible death.
One-- one just-- the place inside looked-- there was no place to-- to sit down.
Everybody was one on top of the other.
And some of them held themselves other.
You had to force it to separate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what it was.
And I love Jewish reading in-- in Tanach.
As I told you before, I made already a tape, and I made another tape.
But I overheard in this neighborhood somebody made a tape.
So I said, why not me?
I have a story like anybody else.
If-- if they have tapes where I-- I know what-- where they were.
So I have-- I have more stories to tell than they do.
When I called once, it was already time back.
I didn't have no answer from you.
But it came months, months, months went by.
And then a call came to me back.
So I told them, I'm ready.
